Appendix 1
Investigating thin and anorexic  
guinea pigs
John Chitty
Thin and/or anorexic guinea pigs are a frequent presentation  approach is therefore useful in order to maximize the 
in practice. They can be difficult to diagnose and treat as  chances of a correct diagnosis and thus correct treatment 
there are a wide range of differential diagnoses and clinical  or management.
signs are often generalized and non-specific. A logical 
STEP 1 Assess the guinea pig’s eating habits
■  If the guinea pig is eating normally, this indicates a thin guinea pig – go to Step 2a.
■  If the guinea pig is not eating, is eating less than normal or is eating selectively, this indicates an anorexic guinea pig – go to Step 2b.
STEP 2a Obtain a history for the thin guinea pig
■  Is the weight loss recent or has it occurred over a long period of time?
■  How old is the guinea pig?
■  Older guinea pigs are more at risk of hyperthyroidism, neoplasia, heart disease, renal disease and arthritis.
■  Middle-aged animals are more at risk of dental-related problems, temporomandibular joint problems and middle ear disease.
■  Younger animals are potentially more at risk of social group problems and infectious diseases (e.g. yersiniosis).
■  How long has the animal been owned? 
■  Stress and mixing problems should be considered.
■  Housing – are the guinea pigs housed indoors or outdoors?
■  Indoor housing: potentially more areas for group conflict over food.
■  Outdoor housing: in cold weather, animals may require increased feeding (and there is a greater risk of spoilage and interference by 
wild rodents).
■  Photographs and videos of the housing set-up and companion animals are useful.
■  Faeces – have there been any changes in the size, shape, consistency or volume of the faeces? Has there been an increase or 
decrease in the frequency of defecation?
■  This is an important indicator of gastrointestinal motility.
■  Urine – have there been any changes in the colour or volume of urine? Are there any signs of pain on urination?
■  Diet – have there been any changes in the diet provided to the guinea pig?
■  Problems with food quality, quantity, calorie density and incompatibility/dislike should be considered.
■  Have other clinical signs been reported?
■  This may include drooling, oculonasal discharge, breathing problems, urine soaking, polyuria/polydipsia and masses (visualized or 
palpated).
■  Once a full history has been obtained, go to Step 3a.
STEP 2b Obtain a history for the anorexic guinea pig
■  How long has the animal been anorexic?
■  Is the animal completely anorexic or showing reduced eating?
■  Is the animal trying to eat or not showing any interest in food?
■  Is the animal selectively eating?
■  Note that the preceding three questions are vital as they indicate whether the apparent anorexia is more likely to be due to dental 
disease, systemic disease or other diseases of the skull that result in reduced mastication.
■  Is the animal losing weight/condition?
■  How old is the guinea pig?
■  Older guinea pigs are more at risk of hyperthyroidism, neoplasia, heart disease, renal disease and arthritis.
■  Middle-aged animals are more at risk of dental-related problems, temporomandibular joint problems and middle ear disease.
■  Younger animals are potentially more at risk of social group problems and infectious diseases (e.g. yersiniosis).
■  How long has the animal been owned? Are there any new guinea pigs in the group? Have there been any changes in the group 
dynamic? Are other guinea pigs affected? Have there been any changes in husbandry?
■  Diet – has the food provided changed recently? Is the owner using large bags of food and near the end of a bag?
■  Food spoilage or incompatibility can be significant factors.
■  Faeces – have there been any changes in the size, shape, consistency or volume of the faeces? Has there been an increase or 
decrease in the frequency of defecation?
■  This is an important indicator of gastrointestinal motility. (Step 2b continues)

BSAVA Manual of Guinea Pigs
STEP 2b Obtain a history for the anorexic guinea pig
(Step 2b continued)
■  Urine – have there been any changes in the colour or volume of urine? Are there any signs of pain on urination?
■  Have other clinical signs been reported?
■  Once a full history has been obtained, go to Step 3b.
STEP 3a Undertake a full examination of the thin guinea pig to decide what further assessments are needed
Note: while dental disease is relatively unlikely in an animal that is not anorexic, it cannot be ruled out without a full oral examination 
under anaesthesia and diagnostic imaging. (Hyporexia can present without anorexia in guinea pigs with dental disease.)
■  Head – assess the eyes and nose for signs of discharge. Are there signs of scratching at the ears? Feel for bumps on the ventral 
mandible and assess the angle the incisors meet at. Palpate for masses along the mandible/maxilla that may indicate dental 
abscessation. If indicators of dental disease are present (note that dental disease is not always obvious above the gum line), a full 
dental assessment may be required – go to Step 4.
■  Body condition score – assess the thinness of the animal and compare with bodyweight. Does the lack of condition affect the 
whole body or only part (e.g. the hindquarters)? If body condition loss only affects part of the body, spinal or pelvic trauma/injury 
should be considered – go to Step 5.
■  Body palpation – assess for the presence of masses. Fine-needle aspirates should be obtained from any masses identified. If an 
abdominal mass is present or pain is indicated in the bladder, go to Steps 5 and 6.
■  Note that ovarian cysts are present in >80% of sows >18 months old and may or may not be clinically significant.
■  Auscultation – assess the heart and lungs. Lung noise may indicate chronic respiratory disease, as may a lack of chest compres-
sibility and obvious dyspnoea. The heart should be carefully assessed for dysrhythmias and soft murmurs. If abnormalities are 
detected upon auscultation, go to Step 6. If the heart rate is extremely rapid in an older animal, go to Step 7.
■  Skin lesions/pruritus – assess the skin. The presence of extensive crusting and pruritus may indicate mite infestation. Skin 
thickening and hair loss may be signs of cutaneous neoplasia. Skin scrapes and biopsy should be performed if findings are 
inconclusive.
■  Ventral neck – palpate to assess for a thyroid mass. The thyroid gland can be distinguished from lymph nodes as it is relatively 
mobile, usually singular and green fluid may be obtained upon aspiration. If green fluid is found, go to Step 7.
■  Faecal consistency – identify whether there are any changes (especially if the faeces are loose). If changes in consistency are 
found, go to Step 8.
■  If there are no specific findings from the full examination, go to Step 7.
STEP 3b Undertake a full examination of the anorexic guinea pig to decide what further assessments are needed
■  If the animal is trying to eat and rejecting food, if there is selective feeding rejecting harder/fibrous food items, or if the animal is 
taking longer to masticate and/or eating less than normal, skip auscultation and move on to assessing the head – go to Step 5.
■  Auscultation – assess the heart and lungs (if the animal has no interest in food). Lung noise may indicate chronic respiratory 
disease, as may a lack of chest compressibility and obvious dyspnoea. The heart should be carefully assessed for dysrhythmias 
and soft murmurs (the most common sign of heart disease in guinea pigs is anorexia). If abnormalities are detected (or if no other 
findings are detected in middle-aged to older animals), go to Steps 5 and 6. If the heart rate is extremely rapid in an older animal, 
go to Step 7.
■  Head – assess the eyes and nose for signs of discharge. Are there signs of scratching at the ears? Feel for bumps on the ventral 
mandible and assess the angle the incisors meet at. Palpate for masses along the mandible/maxilla that may indicate dental 
abscessation. If indicators of dental disease are present (note that dental disease is not always obvious above the gum line), a full 
dental assessment may be required – go to Step 4.
■  Body condition score – assess the thinness of the animal and compare with bodyweight. If the animal is very thin and anaesthesia 
is required to carry out further investigations, a period of stabilization and assisted feeding may be required before progressing. 
Assess hydration status.
■  Faecal consistency – identify whether there are any changes (especially if the faeces are loose). If changes in consistency are 
found, go to Step 8.
■  Body palpation – assess for the presence of masses. Fine-needle aspirates should be obtained from any masses identified. If an 
abdominal mass is present or pain is indicated in the bladder, go to Steps 5 and 6.
■  Note that ovarian cysts are present in >80% of sows >18 months old and may or may not be clinically significant.
■  If there are no specific findings from the full examination, go to Step 7.
STEP 4 Conduct a dental examination under anaesthesia or sedation
■  Clean the mouth thoroughly and assess all teeth.
■  Conduct radiography and/or computed tomography (CT) of the skull to assess the tooth roots, temporomandibular joints and tympanic 
bullae (include at least five views) – go to Step 5.
STEP 5 Conduct survey radiography
Note: CT is an alternative option but survey radiographs are faster and more economical. CT can be used to assess particular areas in 
detail or as part of a pre-surgical examination.
■  Take lateral and dorsoventral radiographs with the forelimbs extended cranially and the hindlimbs extended caudally.
■  Assess renal shape and outline – if any changes are identified, go to Steps 7 and 9.
STEP 6 Conduct thoracic and abdominal ultrasonography
Note: ultrasonography can usually be performed with the animal conscious.
■  Consider biopsy of suspicious masses, which may be indicated.

Appendix 1 • Investigating thin and anorexic guinea pigs
STEP 7 Conduct blood sampling
■  Full haematology profile – assess for renal and hepatic function, as well as hepatocellular damage.
■  If the animal is >3 years old, include total thyroxine measurement.
STEP 8 Conduct faecal cytology
Note: parasitism is extremely rare.
■  Cytology is indicated if faeces are loose; examine bacterial morphology and assess the faeces for the presence of a large number of 
yeast organisms.
STEP 9 Conduct urine sampling
■  Profile – assess specific gravity, cytology and protein levels. Note that a urine protein:creatinine ratio is not necessarily reliable, but the 
presence of protein is significant.
